I'm looking at a beautiful Chris. Okay.
Winston, are you there?

Pure. Hi, um, welcome to the Regulations Review Committee. Is that Winston? It is me indeed. This is Winston. Thank you very much for coming along. Um, would you mind turning on your video? I shall try. Thank you. Um, just while you're doing that, my name's Arina Williams.

I'm the chair of the Regulations Review Committee. Um, we are going to give you 10 minutes to present to us, um, about your complaint. Would encourage you to leave five minutes if you can. Four questions. I'm sure members would like to engage with what you've got to say. We can't see the video, but, um, go ahead and just, um, make your submission orally.

Thank you. Can you see me? No, just go ahead though. Okay. Yes. My name is Winston Marsh. I'm a resident in Gisborne Pathy. And I would like to thank you for hearing me today and I'll try and be brief. I understand the purpose of your committee.

Um, Madam Chair is to ensure regulations that are made by various bodies are fair, consistent, and within the powers granted by Parliament. And the Commerce Commission has a similar role in that it promotes competition to protect consumers from monopolistic trading and predatory pricing.

So I'm here today to suggest the commission has dropped the ball here because it's granted backdated permission to the first flight company to collect over a million dollars from local gisborne y raw households and small business. And the purpose of the payment being taken through the electricity account is to reimburse first light.

Now owned by Cap International for costs is incurred repairing its power lines, the network following the cyclone known as Gabriel. And I submit those costs apart and parcel of the rough and tumble of running a business. My son's horticultural land was inundated and severely affected and he, like the other 30,000 or so of us were without electricity for days.

And I interpolate here that the commission had already waived the, um, excessive time we were without power. Can I just check? You can hear me okay? Yes, we can hear you. Thank you. Um, the, the daily rate charge that's in comes from the retailer of course, but it's for the supplier, um, that continued and it's over a dollar a day.

Um, and so I'm submitting that all of these costs that the company has incurred, it just part of the rough and tumble of running that business. And so we are actually being asked to act as insurer for the company when in fact it should have its own insurance and very possibly haters International does have international, um, cover, but who would know?

Now, the purpose, as you will know of the Commerce Act is in for this part is set out in section 52, big A of the ACT and is quotation marks to promote the long term benefit of consumers in regulated markets. So I look at 52 A and it says, well, I'm not going to read you the whole section 52 A.

I'm sure you can. I'm sure that you guys know what that's all about, but certainly 52 1D is to limit the ability to extract excess of profits. And, and I'll, I'll leave that section open, but there's nothing in those purposes. Um, in 52 a of the act to suggest the consumers may be charged for the cost of the impact on the income stream of a monopoly lines company from a natural disaster.

Indeed, the words of Section 52 A actually indicate the contrary. Now, um, be more specific and brief. I would like to comment on the Gazette notice, which is, which is, um, delegated legislation or secondary legislation that the committee is responsible for.

And I'm holding the Gazette notice in my hand, and you can't see it because I haven't got my camera on. It's an old machine, but it's ended up, uh, commerce Commission notification of amendment to electricity distribution services, default path price, Python quality Determination 20 20 20 19.

And as a reference NZ C 21 isn't short, it's an amendment to price parameters. Am I shouting now? No, no. We can hear expired 31 March, 2025 gone. And the first words of the notice are under Section 52 P, which section purportedly cloaks the commission's authority to introduce a catastrophic allowance into the previously set consumer charges as previously published in the Gisbon Herald public Notices.

So, so I think, um, you've, um, made your point clear. I think I'll open up now for questions from the committee. Um, we've only got three more minutes, um, so I'll make those brief. Does anyone have any, okay. Um, No. Do you, do you want to tell us some more information or do you want questions?

Yes, I, I really should because, um, okay. The, the notice itself is buried into attachment A on page seven of the reconsideration of the default path by the Commerce Commission for Cyclone Gabriel catastrophic event request dated one May 25.

And the application is for relief by the company and it says we determine the price path under the Methodologies Act, which is Commerce Commission's own legislation on a Forecast X anti. Now, what sort of words is X anti this, this isn't very good legislation, X hyphen anti, if it's meant to be Latin, there are no hyphens in Latin.

Um, so it's a made up word in the regulation and that that offends me as well, frankly. And now I'll stop. Fair enough. Um, thank you. Thank you. Um, the, I think the committee would be interested in you talking about what effect this has on you.

How much, um, more will you pay because of this determination? And what does that mean for your household As I, it depends on how long they spread the payments out for. Mm, mm-hmm. As I understand it, they're going to introduce it in 2027. Um, they, they could what they call smooth it, and I suspect they'll be smoothing it to the extent of, in my case, my household $6 a month.

And that's a lot of money on top of the $30 a month that they've just hit us with in so-called Catchups, which all of you will be aware of from your own power accounts. Um, do you mind my asking, uh, what other, so that we understand relatively, what, what do you pay for your power bill on a monthly basis now?

Two or $300. My, my, um, treasurer Oceany, my wife, she does that Good on her Busy. We've probably got time for one more short question. No. Okay. Um, Winston, who Are you talking to please? It's, have I got The whole committee there? Yes, we do have the whole committee.

I'm sorry you can't see us. We are out of time. We, um, want to really thank you for engaging with the committee and back and forth correspondence, but also coming along today. Um, if you would like to share any other information with us, we will be in touch with you to do, to get anything else that we need. Am I unable to stay and watch the Commerce Commission's presentation now?

You are welcome to sit in for ComCom. Um, you'll be, you, uh, you won't be a panelist anymore, so you won't be able to contribute to that. No, but can I see and hear it? I'm not sure what you can see, but yes. Oh, I can see the three of you at the front. Okay. But that's all. Yeah, We'll be we'll be moving on camera so you can see.
Great. Okay. Um, alright, now welcome you to the table. Thank you.

Thanks very much. Um, Adrian, we've got 20 minutes. Um, I'll start the time now, but, um, thank you. Do also just wanna make sure that we Yes. Um, good afternoon, Madam Chair and Madam Deputy Chair and members of the Regulations Review Committee. I'm Adrian Meko, I'm the Chief Executive of the Commerce Commission.

Uh, I'd like to introduce you to Andy Burgess, who's our general manager responsible for infrastructure regulation work, including, um, electricity distribution, service matters, and to, um, Andrew Risely, our general counsel. Um, sitting behind us is, uh, Rachel Coyle, one of our principal advisors who's also been involved in looking at this matter.

And, um, just wanted to, um, say hello to you also, Mr. Um, Morton Hines Marsh, um, and acknowledge you are there and I know, um, Rachel had spoken to you. I'll pass to, um, Andy and Andrew to answer questions, but as you wish to proceed, Madam Chair, um, it might be really, um, contextualized for the committee, how, why this decision was made, um, what a normal decision making process would look like, sort of five year period and how, um, you know, what alternatives you had available.

Um, where we'll also have some specific questions for you about bylaw authorization was the decision made, but let's deal with that a little bit later. Absolutely. Thanks very much. I'll pass over to Andy and Andrew. Thanks very much, Adrian. Um, so I'll, I'll cover the, the general approach and then I'll hand over to Andrew to talk about the legal aspects.

Um, so our, our focus at the commission is on the long-term interest of consumers, and we think that having ERs like this is consistent with that aim. Um, I think in any, or in in lots of economic regulatory regimes around the world where you have a five year period within which we set the maximum revenue that an entity can earn and the minimum level of reliability that they have to deliver, the, the, there has to be some sort of change mechanisms because certain, certain, uh, circumstances change over that period.

And you can have severe weather events such as the cyclone, um, that affected the East Coast. So having ERs is a fairly normal thing in network regulation. And we think that having them is in the consumer interest because, uh, say in the case for catastrophic event, if we didn't have ERs, and probably one of two things would happen, either the regulated entity would price in the risk to the prices that they charge retailers and then, and consumers and consumers might end up paying for that risk, even if the risk doesn't eventuate

or the regulated entity might do nothing. And then you would have a circumstance, say with the cyclone where reliability on the network suffers and they wait until the next regulatory period, the next five year period to try to get the revenue to invest in the network. So we think having this kind of mechanism allows us to scrutinize the expenditure in consumer's interests and make sure consumers get value for money.

We recognize that that unfortunately has a bill impact for people who may already be, be struggling with the size of their electricity bills, but we think it's more efficient and allows us to scrutinize the expenditure in a way which is better than the alternatives. So we think ERs are in consumer's interest.

In the case of this reopener, we did scrutinize the expenditure, uh, first like commissioned an external expert to look at the expenditure and we looked at that report, um, and we consulted before making a final decision. I have to say that having looked at this in the light of a Mr.

Mr. Morton, Hein Marsh's, um, complaint, we do feel that we could do more to bring these sort of decisions to consumer's attention and we think the regulated entities should be doing more. So we, we've made a couple of changes recently, firstly, in guidance to, uh, electricity distribution businesses on these ERs, we set an expectation that they will engage with their communities before submitting to us, and we will look at that level of engagement in making a decision.

And then for our own consultation, so we always consult publicly, but we recognize that consumers aren't necessarily avid watches of our website. Um, we have started using social media to draw attention to catastrophic ERs, and so we did that in, in a recent case with unison. So that's, I guess the policy context where we think ERs are in consumers interest.

I'll hand over to Andrew to, to deal with the legal ethics. Uh, thanks Andy. Uh, I think the most useful contribution I can make is just to ensure that, um, the committee understands the, uh, the way that the, uh, the determinations are made under part four of the Communist Act.

So the foundational rules for part four regulation are in something called the input methodologies. And they're intended to be, uh, principles that are set, that are, that are, that are long-term endurable. They, they deal with things like, uh, the weighted average cost of capital, but also things like ERs and they're intended as a, as a set of, of rules that are, that are enduring.

Um, and then every five years we set under, uh, the, um, under section 52 P of the Commerce Act, uh, uh, a default price quality path or where a, where a electricity business applies for a customized price quality path. And that's the revenue requirement and the quality standards for, for a, for a five year period.

Uh, the, um, I think it's important to appreciate that the, one of the other things that the IMS do, sorry, the input methodologies do, um, is, uh, is prescribe how different price quality periods interrelate. Um, so a price quality period for five years is not a hermetically sealed box.

Um, one of the things that can happen during that five year period is the business, the electricity distribution, electricity distribution business may over recover or under recover against the revenue requirement that's in that period, and that there's something called the washup mechanism that effectively pulls forward and under recovery and over recovery and, and, and it's recognized in the, in the following period.

Um, and because the reconciliation of that only happens after the end of the preceding period, the, the, the, um, the recognition of that, of that over recovery or under recovery only happens, um, in fact in, in, in either year two or year three of the following period. So they're not, they're not, as I say, hermetically sealed five years there, there's a degree of flow of revenue that happens.

Um, what happens, um, with the DPP is the revenue requirement and the quality standards are reset, but, um, but there's already, even in the absence of, of something like a reopener, there's already quite a lot of flow of revenue that happens from one period to the other. Mm. Um, you'll have seen in the correspondence to the committee from, um, from our chief exec that, um, we say the, the effect of the, I think it's important for the committee to appreciate that the effect of the reopener is prospective.

So in no case is the electricity bill that someone has already paid in a previous period reopened, and the electricity company doesn't come and say, right, well we've had the, we've had a cyclone and, um, we're now gonna say that in, in, um, period X, you should have paid more than you did that both in terms of the revenue that the electricity distribution business can collect and the impact on bills that happens prospectively.

Um, got a question from Miles Anderson. I've got some. So, um, the catastrophic event allowance, why are so, and, and, and, you know, first thought is one example, but there's lots of up down the country, why so many, uh, companies, uh, uh, over the period of the last 20 years applying for this or, uh, carve out when, you know, in my area, for example, I could count probably eight catastrophic events really, that have, um, upset them a distribution grid in the last 50 years

when they obviously, uh, they haven't regularly and the companies don't seem to be keeping enough retained earnings to cover that, and they're using the consumer as the, um, insurance. So my question I guess is why are we allowing that to happen?

Because I'm sure the, um, companies will be getting somewhere between eight and 12% return on investment for them to retain some earnings full catastrophic bid that regularly You want me to Yep. So if I, if I can answer that one. So I think on the catastrophic reopener, the first light application is the first one since the reopener mechanism was introduced In 2020.

Yeah, so we've only had two applications so far. One from firstlight and one from Unison, both for the, the cyclone. Um, there are other ERs which regulated entities, uh, can apply to use. Um, we, I mean, the reopening is called catastrophic because it's obviously a very severe weather event, so it's not designed to cut that just short periods of adverse weather.

We would expect the entities to manage those and we set minimum reliability standards, and so we expect them to comply with those standards and to deal with, uh, the norm if you'd like. These three openers are for exceptional events. Um, I think in terms of why, say the owners shouldn't be picking up the tab for this kind of event, I think if, if the regime worked like that, I suspect the owners would price in the, their take on the likelihood of one of these events.

And so consumers would pay more on an ongoing basis. Having a reopener means that we can look at the specific circumstances, they don't need to price it in, we can look at it, we can decide what's reasonable and we can decide what represents value for money for consumers. So we think it, it is actually in, in consumers better interest to have a reopener than for the, the owners to price it in to, to the ongoing, uh, prices that they value.

Right. So I'm guessing, so before 2020, what was the regime like then? When We had some ERs, I, um, and they would cover particular circumstances, but I think we, we introduced the catastrophic reopener on the basis that, you know, New Zealand does have very severe weather events from time to time.

We felt it was necessary following consultation. So is it harder to ensure for those companies for this sort of thing, um, then, um, having DISA allowance? Yeah, so I think, I think it's quite hard to ensure, so I'm getting this anecdotally, but I think it's quite hard to ensure there's the basic poles and wires.

I think bigger equipment such as transformers can be insured, unison, we know had some insurance and we, we deduct the amount they're getting from insurance from the amount that they can recover from consumers. Uh, first flight didn't have any insurance. Um, thank you. This has been useful. Um, The first question is about, um, is this the appropriate model for these lines companies that are now privately owned?

Like we went from a model of having, you know, a regulated price path, price quality path for essentially community trust owned assets. This was owned by Eastland Group. Is this still appropriate when the group of 23 made a distribution of more than 50 million? Um, from, from this s So, um, the legislation basically determines whether an entity is subject to a price pass, is if the entity is, is con consumer owned, it's exempt from having a price pass just subject to information disclosure.

Um, if it's not consumer owned under the legislation, it has to be price regulated. Um, I think sort of looking to the future, we would like more flexibility to bring people in and out price regulation. Uh, and I think generally we don't think that the ownership model dictates really how complex an entity is or whether it needs greater scrutiny.

It's a lot of it's to do with the circumstances and the management of the entity. Um, but as it stands it's all determined by legislation. Well, um, so the information that you shared with us about the, um, the financial flow is obviously really relevant. Um, but that's not necessarily how the, uh, legislation reads.

Um, it seems like within the period, um, that, um, you know, we've got a re regulated monopoly here that would spend on US assets within that period and recover within that period for that spending. Um, this seems to have been, um, a decision that had retrospective that was made retrospectively that didn't have retrospective impact, but still doesn't seem to be duly authorized.

Where does that leave, um, the, um, legal standing of the decision? Well, I think we would say that it is duly authorized. Yeah. Okay. Um, but, um, by sections 52, well by the input methodologies I'm looking at 52 and I'm gonna need some help, but it's quite a basic section.

Yeah. Um, so you think that because section 52 empowers the ims, that the IMS can essentially just say you can accommodate the fact that there will be financial flows between both years despite sort of absolutely being designed in a, in a box Here? Well, I don't, I don't, I would say, and I think you know, that the input methodologies have been in place since 2012.

Yeah. Um, and they are intended to be the foundational, um, document that kind of governs how electricity lines are, are, are regulated on an enduring basis. Yeah. And the fact of there being financial flows between, um, periods has been true from the beginning.

Yeah. So, um, Entirely makes sense to me. Yeah. Um, and, and, and, and, and if you think about it in a way, um, it's unsurprising in that if you had, if they were hermetically sealed five year periods, the catastrophic event happened two minutes.

So, so our, so our, our period, our DPP, sorry, default press quality periods typically start for electricity on, on the 1st of April. Um, so if you had an earthquake at 11:58 PM on the 31st of March, it would be odd if, if, if that had a different treatment to an earthquake that happened at, at 12:02 AM on the 1st of April.

So, so to a certain extent, the fact that there being some kind of a reconciliation that you have to do between periods is, um, I, I, I think just an, a nature of the fact that this is a, this is a rating that, that that's enduring. Um, we do talk in the correspondence from the chief, from the chief executive about the fact that, uh, the, the, the, the reason that the reopener happened and the way that it did was because the input methodologies at the time when the catastrophic event happened effectively hemmed in the ability of our commissioners to,

um, to reopen the reopen, uh, DPP, the, the de default price quality period, um, in which the decision was made, um, at, at the time the decision was made. Yeah. Mm-hmm. So, um, we've looked at the, um, the input methodologies actually prior to the complaint coming in, um, and the, the input methodologies have actually been amended so that there is more flexibility for, for the commission to be able to effectively reopened at the point where the, the decision has been taken rather than to reopen a previous period.

Yeah. That's, um, but, but it is important to say that that whatever happens, the, the impact on bills is prospective. Um, and, and, um, if the input methodologies had been structured in the way that they are now, back then precisely the same financial impact would've happened.

So, so there's not, so yeah. So what advice did you give at the time on why the bills couldn't be prospective in the new period? Why did it have to happen in the previous period? Because at the time when the application came in, um, and, and the, at the time the, the application came in and when the, the catastrophic event had happened, our input methodologies, which we, you know, we do have to adhere to, um, said that, that the price quality period three couldn't be reopened within that, within the, the window of time

that that would've been available. Yeah. Yeah. No, my question is why wasn't your advice changed? The amount in default price, um, period for Yes. Be be because that wasn't available, right? Yeah. But you've now made that, but it is Now it it is now, yes. Be because the input methodologies subsequent to that have been changed so that they would would've allowed it. Yes. Okay.

Um, and you know, and I understand we, it's, it's, it's a, it's a really difficult one and we need to have good networks and we need to have ability to pay for that.

Mm-hmm. Um, but this, this does, you know, any new leader, you have the knowledge that it does have some retrospective effect, but I'm still not quite clear in my mind how those sections, he interpret those to enable retrospectively when it's not specifically, um, uh, defined. Yeah. Those sections, I don't think I, I agree.

Yep. So, so, uh, so is the question is does the legislation permit the I methodologies to do this? Yeah. To do Because we think it, perhaps it should. Well, perhaps it should. Well, there's the question about whether it should, I guess in, in the sense of everything being guided by 52 A.

Yeah. Um, I suppose 52 T talks about regulatory processes and rules, such as, it doesn't say the only regulatory rules and processes you can have are those. So to a certain extent, 52 t we regard as a, as a, as a list of things that can appear in the input methodologies rather than an exclusive list that says only these things can.

Um, but yeah, I, I, I think the, the, the, the correspondence from the chief executive kind of explains the ary that our commissioners were in, um, in that at the particular point where the, the catastrophic event happened, the input methodologies only really allowed commissioners one of two options.

And they've taken the, the and, and the input. I, I guess the input methodologies have, have been set. The, the commissioners, I think understandably regard themselves as bound by them. Yeah. I, I guess I, I understand suppose of the interpretation statutes and, and, um, and, and, and there's some, these, the, you know, it's a real policy logic to it.
